concepts.title: Interference of Light
concepts.conceptBody:
Interference of Light

The term interference refers to any situation in which two or more waves overlap in space. When this occurs, the displacement at any point at any instant of time is governed by the principle of superposition which states :

"When two or more waves overlap, the resultant displacement at any point and at any instant may be found by adding the instantaneous displacements that would be produced at any point by the individual waves if each were present alone"

let the two waves be :

$\begin{align} & {{\text{y}}_{1}}={{\text{A}}_{1}}\sin \omega \text{t } \\ & {{\text{y}}_{2}}={{\text{A}}_{2}}\sin (\omega \text{t}+\phi ) \\ \end{align}$

Where $\phi $ is the phase difference

$\begin{align} & y={{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{y}}_{2}} \\ & =\text{A}\sin (\omega \text{t}+\delta ) \end{align}$

Where $\text{A}=\text{A}_{1}^{2}+\text{A}_{2}^{2}+2{{\text{A}}_{1}}{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi$

and $\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} I= & {{I}_{1}}+{{I}_{2}}+2\sqrt{{{I}_{1}}{{I}_{2}}}\text{cos}\phi ~\left\{ \text{as } I\propto {{A}^{2}} \right\} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Condition for Maximum Intensity
concepts.conceptBody:
Condition for Maximum Intensity

$\begin{align} & \cos \phi =1\quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi =\pm 2\text{n}\pi \\ & \phi =\text{n}\lambda \quad \text{ Where }\quad \text{n}=0,1,2 \\ \end{align}$

And $\mathrm{A}_{\max }^2=(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A})^2$

$I_{\max }=\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2$

also, $\phi =\left( \frac{2\pi }{\lambda } \right)\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }$

Where $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }$ is the path difference

concepts.title: Conditions for Interference
concepts.conceptBody:
Conditions for Interference 

In order to obtain a sustained (permanent or stable) and observable interference pattern, the following conditions must be fulfilled.

  1. Sources must be Coherent : In order to produce a stable interference patter the individual waves must maintain a constant phase relationship with one another, i.e., the two interfering sources must emit waves having a constant phase difference between them. If the phase difference between two sources does not remain constant then the places of maxima and minima shift. In case of mechanical waves it is possible to keep a constant phase relationship between two different sources. But in case of light two different light sources can't be coherent. This is because of the way light is emitted. In ordinary light sources, atoms gain excess energy by thermal agitation or by impact with accelerated electrons. An atom that is 'excited' in such a way begins to radiate energy and continues until it has lost all the energy it can, typically in a time of the order of ${{10}^{-8}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ s}$. The many atoms in a source ordinarily radiate in an unsynchronized and random phase relationship, and the light that is emitted from two such sources has no definite phase relationship. Hence, to obtain a stable interference in light a single source is split into two coherent sources. Any random phase change in the source affects these secondary sources equally and does not change their relative phase. Laser light is much more coherent than ordinary light.
  2. Same frequency or wavelength : Phase relationship between two waves can be kept constant only when their frequencies are same. Thus we can say, that two coherent sources must have the same frequency.
  3. Equality of amplitudes : The amplitudes of two interfering waves should be equal or approximately equal. Otherwise the difference between the intensities of maxima’s and minima’s will be too small and the contrast will be poor. Maximum contrast is, however obtained when ${{A}_{1}}={{A}_{2}}$, because then minimum intensity will be zero.
  4. Both should be travelling in same direction

concepts.title: Constructive and Destructive Interference
concepts.conceptBody:
Constructive and Destructive Interference

We consider two identical sources of monochromatic waves ${{S}_{1}}$ and ${{S}_{2}}$ along the $y$-axis, equidistant from the origin.

 

Consider a point ' $\text{a}$ ' on X-axis. From symmetry the two distances from ${{\text{S}}_{1}}$ to a $\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{S}}_{2}}$ to a are equal; waves from the two sources thus require equal times to travel to a. Hence waves that leaves ${{\text{S}}_{1}}$ and ${{S}_{2}}$ in phase arrive at a in phase. The two waves add and the total amplitude at ' $a$ ' is twice the amplitude of each individual waves. This is true for any point on the x-axis.

Similarly, the distance from ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ to point $\text{b}$ is exactly two wavelengths greater than the distance from ${{S}_{1}}$ to $b$. A wave rest from ${{S}_{1}}$ arrives at $b$ exactly two cycles earlier than a emitted at the same time from ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ and again the two waves arrive in phase. As at point $\text{O}$, the total amplitude is the sum of the amplitudes of the waves from ${{S}_{1}}$ and ${{S}_{2}}$.

In general, when waves from two or more sources arrive at a point in phase, the amplitude of resultant wave is the sum of the amplitudes of the individual wave; the individual waves reinforce each other. This is called constructive interference. Let the distance from ${{S}_{1}}$ to any point $P$ be ${{r}_{1}}$ and let the distance from ${{\text{S}}_{2}}$ to $\text{P}$ be ${{\text{r}}_{2}}$.

For constructive interference to occur at $\text{P}$, the path difference ${{r}_{2}}-{{r}_{1}}$ between two waves must be an integral multiple of the wavelength $\lambda $;

\({{\text{r}}_{2}}-{{\text{r}}_{1}}=\text{m}\lambda \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{m}=0,\pm 1,\pm 3\ldots \right)\) …(i)

At point $\text{C},{{\text{r}}_{2}}-{{\text{r}}_{1}}=-2.5\lambda $, which is a half-integral number of wavelengths waves from the two sources arrive at point $\text{C}$ exactly a half cycle out of phase. A crest of one wave arrives at the same time a trough of the other wave. the resultant amplitude is the difference between the two individual amplitudes. If the individual amplitudes are equal, then the total amplitude is zero. This cancellation or partial cancellation of the individual waves is called destructive interference.

$\text{ }{{\text{r}}_{2}}-{{\text{r}}_{1}}=\left( \text{m}+\frac{1}{2} \right)\lambda \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{m}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\pm 3\ldots \right)$ ...(ii)

The path difference at $\text{C}$ satisfies with $\text{m}=-3$.

concepts.title: Optical Path Difference
concepts.conceptBody:
Optical Path Difference 

When a light travels from rarer to denser then there is a phase difference of $\pi $ and path difference of $\frac{\lambda }{2}$.

Now, when a glass slab is placed in its path, then path difference can be: 

$\frac{\text{A'B'}{{}_{\text{optical }}}}{\text{C}}=\frac{\text{d}-\text{t}}{\text{C}}+\frac{\text{t}}{\text{C}/\mu }=\frac{\text{d}+\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{t}}{\text{C}}$

$\therefore \text{ A'B}_{\text{optical }}^{'}=\text{d}+\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{t }\left\{ \text{A'B'} \right.$ optical is the air equivalent path of light$\}$ $\text{ A}{{\text{B}}_{\text{original }}}=\text{d}$

$\therefore$ path difference $=\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{t}$
